1: hălōʾ-ṣāḇāʾ lěʾěnôš ʿal-ʾāreṣ waḵîmê śāḵîr yāmāyw: 'Has not man a hard service on earth, and are not his days like the days of a hired hand?' Ṣāḇāʾ (hard service, military conscription, compulsory service) is the term used for the Levites' temple service (Numbers 4:3, 23, 30, 35, 39, 43) and for military service (Numbers 1:3; Job 31:3). When applied to human existence generally, it frames life itself as involuntary conscription into a campaign with no discharge. Śāḵîr (hired laborer, day-worker) adds the dimension of economic servitude: both the soldier and the day-worker live entirely at the disposal of another, waiting for the day to end. Job's first verse of his divine soliloquy frames the entire human condition as servitude — a searing counter to the creation theology that calls humanity the crown of creation (Genesis 1:26-28; Psalms 8:4-8).
2: kəʿeḇeḏ yišʾap̄ ṣēl wəḵəśāḵîr yəqawweh pōʿolô: 'Like a slave who longs for the shadow, and like a hired hand who looks for his wages.' ʾĀḇeḏ (slave) and śāḵîr (hired laborer) continue the labor metaphor. The slave longs for ṣēl (shade — the midday rest that provides relief from the killing sun) and the day-worker longs for pōʿăl (wages — the moment of completion and reward). Both are endurance-under-duress: the slave endures the heat for the shade; the laborer endures the work for the wage. Job's comparison: his life is exactly this — endurance waiting for a relief that does not come (Job 7:3: months of emptiness).
3: kēn huḥlaḥtî yarhê-šāwəʾ wəlêlôṯ ʿāmāl sippərû-lî: 'so I am allotted months of emptiness, and nights of misery are apportioned to me.' The passive verb huḥlaḥ (allotted, from nāḥal, to inherit/receive as a portion) makes YHWH implicit subject: Job has been given an inheritance of šāwəʾ (emptiness, vanity, futility — the same root as Ecclesiastes' šāwəʾ). The months and nights are precisely counted: sippərû-lî (are numbered for me, apportioned to me by divine accounting). The verse is a deliberate echo of the creation week's structure (numbered days) but applied to suffering rather than good work — Job's existence is a creation-week of misery.
4: ʾim-šāḵaḇtî wəʾāmartî māṯay ʾāqûm ûmiḏḏad-ʿereḇ wəśāḇaʿtî nəḏuḏîm ʿăḏê-nešep̄: 'When I lie down I say, "When shall I arise?" But the night is long, and I am full of tossing till the dawn.' The daily cycle of hope and disappointment: lying down triggers the wish for morning (māṯay ʾāqûm, when shall I rise?); but the evening (ʿereḇ) brings nəḏuḏîm (tossing, restlessness — from nāḏaḏ, to flee/wander) until dawn (nešep̄). Sleep, the natural human refuge from suffering, is denied. This insomnia-of-suffering becomes a liturgical image in the Psalms: 'in the night his song is with me' (Psalms 42:8) and 'I call out by day but you do not answer, and by night, but I find no rest' (Psalms 22:2).
5: lāḇaš bəśārî rimmāh wəḡûš ʿāp̄ār ʿôrî rāgaʿ wayyimʾās: 'My flesh is clothed with worms and dirt; my skin hardens, then breaks out afresh.' The physical description of Job's illness: rimmāh (worms, maggots — associated with bodily decomposition, Exodus 16:24; Isaiah 14:11; Job 66:24) clothing the flesh while living; gûš ʿāp̄ār (crusted with dirt/dust) and the skin cycle of hardening (rāgaʿ, to harden/close) and breaking open (māʾas, here: to run or break out afresh — disputed). The language anticipates the resurrection: the body being clothed with worms will one day be clothed with incorruption (1 Corinthians 15:53-54). The same Hebrew verb lāḇaš (clothed with) connects the present horror to the eschatological hope: what is now clothed with worms will be clothed with immortality.
6: yāmay qallû miʾāreg wayyiḵlû bəʾep̄es tiqwāh: 'My days are swifter than a weaver's shuttle and come to their end without hope.' ʾĀreg (weaver's shuttle — the wooden implement that passes the weft thread through the warp at high speed) is one of three rapid-motion images Job uses for the passing of life (cf. Job 9:25-26: swifter than a runner, swift as papyrus boats, as an eagle swooping). The shuttle moves so quickly the eye can barely follow it. But the modifier is devastating: bəʾep̄es tiqwāh (with the ending of hope, without hope) — the swift passage of days is not the harmless speed of a full life but the exhaustion of hope. ʾĚp̄es (nothing, ceasing) qualifies tiqwāh (hope) as entirely spent.
7: zəḵōr kî-rûaḥ ḥayyāy lōʾ-ṯāšûḇ ʿênî lirʾôṯ ṭôḇ: 'Remember that my life is a breath; my eye will never again see good.' The address shifts to God: zəḵōr (remember!) is the imperative plea of the lament psalms (Psalms 25:6, 7; Job 74:2, 18, 22; Job 89:47, 50; Job 109:14; Lamentations 5:1) — calling YHWH to act by recalling the covenant. Rûaḥ ḥayyāy (breath of my life, spirit of my life) echoes the creation-breath of Genesis 2:7 (nišmaṯ ḥayyîm). The plea grounds divine attention in the brevity of human life: 'Remember that I am a breath before you act.' The eye not seeing good (ṭôḇ) echoes the creation narrative's 'God saw that it was good' — Job is experiencing un-creation.
8: lōʾ-ṯəšûrənî ʿên rōʾî ʿênêḵā bî wəʾênennî: 'The eye of him who sees me will behold me no more; while your eyes are on me, I shall be gone.' The verse has a double edge: the human eye (ʿên rōʾî, the eye of the one who sees me) will see him no more at death; but also: God's eyes (ʿênêḵā, your eyes) are fixed on Job — and if God does not act before Job dies, the opportunity for vindication is gone. The theological claim: the window for divine response is brief. Death is not the problem solved but the deadline missed. The urgency of the divine address in Job 7:7-21 is entirely shaped by this: Job is dying, and God must act now or not at all.
9: kālāh ʿānān wayyelāḵ kēn yôrēḏ šəʾôl lōʾ yaʿăleh: 'As the cloud fades and vanishes, so he who goes down to Sheol does not come up.' The cloud analogy (ʿānān kālāh wayyelāḵ, cloud that wastes away and goes) is an image of irreversible disappearance — the morning mist burned off by the sun. Šəʾôl (the realm of the dead) is the destination from which there is no return (lōʾ yaʿăleh, does not come up). Job's view here is entirely conventional pre-resurrection — death is final, the realm of the shades is one-directional. The NT's answer is Easter: 'But God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it' (Acts 2:24). Jesus's descent to and ascent from the place of the dead (šəʾôl in the OT, Hades in the LXX) is the direct reversal of the 'does not come up' of Job 7:9.
10: lōʾ-yāšûḇ ʿôḏ liḇêṯô wəlōʾ-yakkirennû ʿôḏ məqômô: 'he returns no more to his house, nor does his place know him anymore.' The permanence of death is extended to social erasure: the dead man's bêṯ (house/household) and māqôm (place) no longer recognize him. Nāḵar (to recognize, know by recognition) is used of social recognition — the dead man's household has moved on, his place is taken. This contrasts with Job 19:25-27 where Job's hope is for post-mortem vindication — here Job is focused on the urgency of divine action before that erasure occurs. The two passages together show Job's oscillation between despair (Job 7) and faith (Job 19).
11: gam-ʾănî lōʾ ʾeḥśōk-pî ʾăḏabbərāh bəṣar rûḥî ʾāśîḥāh bəmar nap̄šî: 'Therefore I will not restrain my mouth; I will speak in the anguish of my spirit; I will complain in the bitterness of my soul.' The defiant turn: the brevity of life (Job 7:6-10) justifies unrestrained complaint. Lōʾ ʾeḥśōk (I will not restrain, from ḥāśaḵ = to withhold, spare) is the inverse of Eliphaz's call to patience. Ṣar rûḥî (narrowness/anguish of my spirit) and mar nap̄šî (bitterness of my soul) combine the physical sensation of choking constriction with the taste-metaphor of bitter suffering that appeared in the mallow-juice verse (Job 6:7). The complaint that follows (Job 7:12-21) is addressed directly to God — a lament of forensic intensity.
12: hăyām-ʾănî ʾim-tannîn kî-ṯāśîm ʿālay mišmār: 'Am I the sea, or a sea monster, that you set a guard over me?' Yām (sea — the cosmic sea of chaos in Canaanite mythology, the watery deep of Genesis 1:2) and tannîn (sea monster/dragon — used in Psalms 74:13; Job 89:10; Isaiah 27:1; 51:9 for the chaos creature defeated by YHWH at creation) are the mythological entities that require divine restraint. The theological implication: YHWH treats Job as though he were a chaos-threat requiring constant surveillance (mišmār, guard, watch). Job is not a cosmic monster — he is a suffering man. The accusation is that God's response to Job is categorically disproportionate: one does not keep a guard over a sick man as though he were the primordial sea.
13: kî-ʾāmartî těnaḥăménî ʿarśî yiśśāʾ bəśîḥî miškaḇî: 'When I say, "My bed will comfort me, my couch will ease my complaint."' Job reports the broken logic of the sufferer: the bed (ʿereś, couch/bed) was expected to provide comfort (nāḥam, the verb of consolation used throughout the psalms and prophets). The word śîḥ (complaint, meditation, speech — the same root as the Psalms' śîaḥ, 'pouring out my complaint to YHWH') shows that Job is not merely resting but lament-praying. But the bed does not bring comfort; instead (Job 7:14) God terrifies him there. The expected sanctuary of sleep has been converted into a site of divine assault.
14: waḥittaṯṯānî ḇaḥălōmôṯ ûmēḥezyônôṯ təḇaʿăṯennî: 'then you scare me with dreams and terrify me with visions.' Bāʿaṯ (to terrify, overwhelm — used of the dread of God's presence, Exodus 15:14, 15; Psalms 18:4; Job 83:15) and ḥāṯaṯ (to shatter, terrify — the antonym of Job's earlier wish for security) describe God's action in the sleep-realm. Dreams and visions (ḥălômôṯ... ḥezyônôṯ) were the standard media of divine communication in the OT (Gen 28; 37; 40-41; Numbers 12:6). Here Job experiences divine communication not as revelation but as terror. The irony is pointed: where the prophets received divine visions with awe and hope, Job receives them with dread — because the God he encounters in sleep is the same God who has destroyed him while awake.
15: watiḇḥar maḥănāq nap̄šî māweṯ miʿaṣmôṯāy: 'so that I would choose strangling and death rather than my bones.' Maḥănāq (strangling) is a desperate image of death by asphyxiation — Job prefers this to continued existence in this body (ʿaṣmôṯ, bones, as the locus of life and vitality, cf. Ezek 37's valley of dry bones). The verse is not a suicidal threat in the modern clinical sense but a theological claim: the severity of divinely-inflicted suffering has crossed the threshold where the self chooses dissolution over continuation. This extreme language is what the friends will criticize as evidence of sin; YHWH's verdict in 42:7 will vindicate Job's honesty.
16: māʾastî lōʾ-lěʿōlām ʾeḥyeh ḥăḏal mimmennî kî-heḇel yāmāy: 'I loathe my life; I would not live forever. Leave me alone, for my days are a breath.' Māʾas (to loathe, despise, reject) applied to his own life (ḥayyê) is Job's most direct expression of self-rejection. The phrase lōʾ lěʿōlām ʾeḥyeh (I will not live forever) is a remarkable anti-immortality claim: Job does not want eternal life in this condition; he wants it to end. Heḇel yāmāy (my days are vanity/breath) echoes Ecclesiastes' opening (hevel havalim) but in Job's mouth it is not philosophical observation but experiential cry. The command ḥăḏal mimmennî (cease from me, leave me alone) is addressed to God: it is simultaneously a prayer (addressed to God) and a desire for divine absence (leave me).
17: māh-ʾěnôš kî-tǝḡaddəlennû wəkî-ṯāśîm ʾēlāyw libbəḵā: 'What is man, that you make so much of him, and that you set your heart on him?' This is the deliberate inversion of Psalms 8:4: 'What is man that you are mindful of him, and the son of man that you care for him?' (Psalms 8:4). The lexical parallels are exact: māh-ʾěnôš (what is man), tāśîm... libbəḵā (set your heart on him = God's attention). But Psalm 8's awe at divine condescension in blessing humanity becomes in Job 7 horror at divine condescension in tormenting humanity. The same act of divine attention — infinitely great God noticing infinitely small man — is experienced as honor in Psalm 8 and as persecution in Job 7. Both are honest responses; the NT resolves the tension in the incarnation: God became man (divine condescension as honor) and suffered (divine condescension as solidarity with Job's terror).
18: wattip̄qəḏennû liḇqārîm lirəgāʿîm tiḇḥānennu: 'visit him every morning and test him every moment?' The divine verbs continue: pāqaḏ (to visit, attend to — in both gracious and punitive senses) and bāḥan (to test, examine, assay — as metal is tested in fire, cf. Psalms 17:3; Job 26:2; Proverbs 17:3; Zechariah 13:9). In the Psalms, both verbs can be positive: 'You have tested my heart, you have visited me by night' (Psalms 17:3) in the context of divine vindication. Here, God's morning visitation (liḇqārîm) and moment-by-moment testing (lirəgāʿîm) are experienced not as divine care but as surveillance and torment. The complaint is that the divine attention appropriate to a covenant examination is being applied continuously without relief — every morning and every moment.
19: kammāh lōʾ-ṯišʿeh mimmennî lōʾ-ṯarp̄ennî ʿaḏ-bəlōʿî ṯūqqî: 'How long will you not look away from me, nor leave me alone till I swallow my spit?' The verb šāʿāh (to look away, to avert the gaze) inverts the standard prayer for divine attention: Job prays for divine inattention as relief. Rāp̄āh (to let go, leave alone — the same root as 'be still and know that I am God,' Psalms 46:10) is the request to cease the divine grip. The hyperbole of 'till I swallow my spit' (literally: till my swallowing of my spittle, a proverbial expression for the briefest possible moment) expresses the desperation: Job cannot get even the most minimal pause in divine attention/assault.
20: ḥāṭāʾṯî māh ʾep̄ʿal lāḵ nōṣēr hāʾāḏām lāmmāh śamtanî ləmiḏʾākekā waʾehyeh ʿālay māśāʾ: 'If I sin, what do I do to you, you watcher of mankind? Why have you made me your mark? Why have I become a burden to you?' Three devastating questions. First: ḥāṭāʾṯî māh ʾep̄ʿal lāḵ (if I have sinned, what have I done to you?) — a theodicy question about whether human sin actually damages God, implying that divine punishment may be disproportionate to any actual harm done. Second: nōṣēr hāʾāḏām (watcher/guardian of man — nōṣēr is used for watchers both benign, Psalms 121:4, and hostile) — addressed directly to God as the one who surveils all human action. Third: śamtanî ləmiḏʾākekā (you have made me your mark/target — mip̄gāʿ or miḏʾāh, the target of an archer) — God is the archer (cf. Job 6:4: arrows of the Almighty) and Job is the target. The lament is not atheism but the most intense form of theism: Job is speaking precisely to the God he believes is doing this.
21: wəlāmmāh lōʾ-ṯiśśāʾ pišʿî wəṯaʿăḇîr ʾet-ʿăwōnî kî-ʿattāh ləʿāp̄ār ʾeškāḇ ûšiḥăraṯanî wəʾênennî: 'Why do you not pardon my transgression and take away my iniquity? For now I shall lie in the earth; you will seek me, but I shall not be.' The final plea of the chapter pivots from complaint to petition: nāśāʾ p̄išaʿ (to lift, carry, forgive transgression — the standard forgiveness formula, Exodus 34:7; Numbers 14:18; Psalms 32:1, 5; Job 85:2) and ʿāḇar ʿāwōn (to pass over iniquity — used by Moses in Exodus 34:7 and by Micah 7:18: 'Who is a God like you, pardoning iniquity and passing over transgression?'). Job is not confessing specific sin but using the full forgiveness vocabulary as a plea for divine release: if there is any sin, forgive it — before the opportunity is gone. The closing line is Job's ultimate forensic weapon: ûšiḥăraṯanî wəʾênennî (you will seek me, but I shall not be). When Job is dead, God will have lost his witness, his case, his defendant. The urgency of the divine response is framed as equally urgent for God's justice as for Job's — a remarkable theological claim that prefigures the NT's account of God's own stake in human redemption.
